title: B. Braun Space Infusion Pump System – Door Open or Door Latch Sensor Errors
description: Troubleshooting guide for B. Braun Space Infusion Pumps displaying Door Open alarms or latch sensor errors, focusing on mechanical door engagement, IV set installation, and latch inspection.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Never troubleshoot the infusion pump while it is connected to a patient receiving medication. If the alarm occurs during therapy, transfer the infusion to a backup pump and confirm medication delivery continues safely before evaluating the device.

steps.title: Verify the Door is Fully Closed
steps.instructions: Open and close the pump door several times to confirm proper engagement. Ensure the latch clicks firmly into place, confirm the door sits flush against the pump housing, and check for gaps between the door and frame. A partially engaged latch can trigger the door sensor.

steps.title: Inspect the IV Set Installation
steps.instructions: Remove the IV set and reinstall it following normal loading procedures. Check for tubing not seated correctly in the pump channel, kinks or twists near the cassette area, and excess tubing interfering with door closure. Improper set placement can prevent the door from fully engaging the latch.

steps.title: Check for Debris in the Door Channel
steps.instructions: Inspect the pump door channel and latch area for obstructions such as dried medication residue, tape fragments, packaging material, or small pieces of tubing or plastic. Even small debris can prevent the latch sensor from activating properly. If debris is present, clean the area with an approved disinfectant wipe and allow it to dry.

steps.title: Inspect the Door Latch Mechanism
steps.instructions: Manually inspect the latch and hinge area. Check for bent latch components, worn latch hooks, or sticky or slow latch movement. The latch should move freely and return smoothly when pressed.

steps.title: Verify the Door Sensor Trigger
steps.instructions: With the IV set removed, close the pump door firmly and observe whether the alarm clears. Gently press the door inward to see if the alarm disappears. If slight pressure clears the alarm, the latch alignment may be worn or misaligned.

steps.title: Perform a Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Power the pump off completely, then power it back on. This allows the system to reset and recheck the door sensor status. If the alarm clears after reboot but returns when the door is used again, the issue is likely mechanical.

steps.title: Test with a Different IV Set
steps.instructions: Install a different manufacturer-approved IV set. Occasionally a damaged or warped cassette can prevent proper door closure.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported the infusion pump repeatedly alarming 'Door Open' even though the door appeared closed during infusion setup.
CCR.Cause: Door latch mechanism showed intermittent engagement due to debris in the latch channel preventing full closure.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned latch area, reinstalled IV set, verified proper door closure, and confirmed alarm cleared. Pump returned to service after functional check.

helpfulDetails: Debris found in latch area (if applicable)
Alarm cleared after cleaning or door adjustment
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: B. Braun Space Infusion Pump System – Occlusion Alarms Despite Free-Flowing Line
description: Troubleshooting guide for B. Braun Space Infusion Pumps generating occlusion alarms even though the IV line appears to be flowing normally.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is safely receiving therapy. If an occlusion alarm is actively interrupting therapy, move the patient to a known-good infusion pump. Do not troubleshoot a device while it is actively being used for patient therapy.

steps.title: Verify the occlusion alarm message and location
steps.instructions: Check whether the pump indicates upstream occlusion or downstream occlusion. This helps determine whether the pump detects pressure before or after the pump mechanism, which guides the rest of troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect the IV tubing path through the pump
steps.instructions: Open the pump door and confirm the tubing is routed correctly in the pump channel, fully seated in the pump track, and not twisted or pinched. Improper tubing placement can create false pressure readings that trigger occlusion alarms.

steps.title: Confirm the correct B. Braun administration set is being used
steps.instructions: Verify that the tubing set is an approved B. Braun Space infusion set and not expired or damaged. Non-approved or incompatible sets can cause incorrect compression in the pumping mechanism, leading to pressure detection errors.

steps.title: Check for partially closed clamps
steps.instructions: Inspect the entire line for roller clamps, slide clamps, and secondary set clamps. Even a partially closed clamp can create enough resistance for the pump to interpret an occlusion.

steps.title: Inspect the line for subtle kinks or compression
steps.instructions: Follow the IV line from bag to patient and look for kinks near the pump door, tubing trapped under bed rails, IV lines caught under equipment wheels, or tight tape near catheter insertion sites. These restrictions may not completely stop flow but can still trigger pressure alarms.

steps.title: Verify the cassette door is fully closed and latched
steps.instructions: Open and re-close the pump door firmly. Check for proper latch engagement and no debris inside the pump channel. A partially latched door may affect occlusion sensor pressure detection.

steps.title: Prime or flush the line
steps.instructions: Air pockets or incomplete priming can affect pressure detection. Confirm the tubing is fully primed and no air bubbles are present near the pump mechanism.

steps.title: Check for downstream resistance at the catheter site
steps.instructions: If safe to assess, confirm the IV catheter is not infiltrated, the dressing is not overly tight, and extension tubing is not kinked. Clinical factors can create resistance that triggers the pump alarm.

steps.title: Test with a known-good administration set
steps.instructions: Replace the tubing set with a new B. Braun set, reprime the line, and restart the infusion. A worn or stiff set can alter compression and cause incorrect occlusion detection.

steps.title: Power cycle the pump
steps.instructions: Turn the pump off and back on to reset the system. This can clear temporary sensor or software faults.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported the infusion pump repeatedly alarming for occlusion even though the IV line appeared to be flowing normally.
CCR.Cause: Tubing set was partially misaligned in the pump channel, causing incorrect pressure detection by the occlusion sensor.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the administration set and verified correct routing. Pump tested and infusion ran normally without additional occlusion alarms.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: B. Braun Space Infusion Pump System – Pump Not Recognized by Space Docking Station
description: Troubleshooting guide for B. Braun Space infusion pumps that are not recognized when inserted into a Space docking station.

steps.title: Confirm the Docking Station Has Power
steps.instructions: Verify the Space docking station is powered. Check for power indicator lights on the docking station, AC power cable securely connected to the wall outlet, and outlet tested with another device if needed. If the dock itself has no power, the pump cannot be recognized.

steps.title: Remove and Reseat the Pump
steps.instructions: Remove the infusion pump from the docking station and reseat it firmly. Ensure the pump slides fully into the docking rails, the latch engages properly, and the pump sits flush with the dock. Partial seating is one of the most common causes of recognition failures.

steps.title: Observe Pump Behavior When Docked
steps.instructions: Watch the pump screen and indicators when inserting it into the dock. Look for charging indicators, communication symbols, or any alert messages. If nothing changes when the pump is inserted, the dock may not be making electrical contact.

steps.title: Inspect Docking Contacts
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the metal contacts on both the pump rear connector and the docking station connector. Look for dust or debris, dried IV fluid contamination, or bent or recessed contacts. If contamination is present, clean gently using approved wipes per facility policy.

steps.title: Test the Pump in Another Dock
steps.instructions: Place the pump into a different docking station that is known to be working. Pump works in another dock: original docking station likely faulty. Pump not recognized in any dock: pump docking interface may be defective. This step helps determine whether the issue is dock-related or pump-related.

steps.title: Test Another Pump in the Same Dock
steps.instructions: Insert a known-good Space infusion pump into the same docking station. Second pump works normally: original pump likely has a docking interface issue. Second pump also fails: docking station may be defective.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Check the pump and dock for mechanical issues. Look for cracked housings, loose docking rails, bent connectors, or signs of fluid intrusion. Mechanical misalignment can prevent proper docking connection.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported the B. Braun Space infusion pump was not recognized when inserted into the docking station and would not charge or communicate with the system.
CCR.Cause: Docking station contacts contaminated with dried IV fluid preventing proper electrical connection.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned docking contacts and reseated pump. Dock recognized pump and charging indicator appeared. Pump returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: B. Braun Space Infusion Pump System - Keypad Not Responding
description: Guide to troubleshoot unresponsive keypad buttons on B. Braun Space Infusion Pumps, focusing on external causes first.

steps.title: Confirm the Pump Is Powered and Fully Booted
steps.instructions: Verify the pump is powered on and has completed its startup process. A pump that is still booting or stuck in a startup state may not respond to keypad input.

steps.title: Check for Active Alarms or Lock Screens
steps.instructions: Some alarm states or lock modes can temporarily restrict input. Clear any alarms and confirm the device is not in a locked or restricted mode.

steps.title: Inspect the Keypad for Contamination
steps.instructions: Look for dried IV fluids, adhesive residue, cleaning chemical buildup, or debris. Sticky residue can prevent buttons from actuating properly or restrict knob movement.

steps.title: Test Individual Buttons
steps.instructions: Press multiple keys individually. Note whether none, some, or all inputs fail to register.

steps.title: Clean the Keypad Surface
steps.instructions: If contamination is present, wipe the keypad area with an approved disinfectant wipe. Ensure no fluid enters around the knob or seams. Allow the surface to fully dry. Sticky residue is a common cause of input failure.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Pump
steps.instructions: Turn the pump off. Remove it from the docking station if docked. Wait approximately 10–15 seconds. Power the unit back on. This clears temporary software or interface lockups.

steps.title: Test Operation Off the Dock
steps.instructions: If the pump was docked, remove it from the docking station. Operate the keypad on battery power. Rarely, docking communication issues can interfere with interface responsiveness.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect for cracked keypad membrane, signs of impact or drop damage, or liquid intrusion near the interface. Mechanical damage often leads to unresponsive inputs.

steps.title: Test Menu Navigation
steps.instructions: Attempt to navigate through menus using the buttons. Confirm whether the device registers scrolling and selection.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing staff reported the infusion pump keypad was not responding when attempting to adjust infusion settings.
CCR.Cause: Testing confirmed keypad inputs were intermittent and failed to register rotation. External contamination ruled out; likely internal keypad/encoder failure.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service and labeled Out of Service. Device sent for repair and replacement pump issued to the unit.

helpfulDetails: Whether pump was docked at time of issue
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: B. Braun Space Infusion Pump System - Battery Not Charging or Rapid Battery Depletion
description: Guide for troubleshooting B. Braun Space Infusion Pumps when the battery fails to charge or depletes rapidly, emphasizing external checks first.

steps.title: Verify Power Source
steps.instructions: Ensure the docking station or wall outlet is supplying power. Test the outlet with a known working device. Reason: A faulty outlet or unplugged docking station is a common cause of battery issues.

steps.title: Inspect Docking Station Connection
steps.instructions: Remove the pump and reseat it on the docking station. Check for debris or misalignment in the contacts. Reason: Poor contact prevents charging even if the pump and station appear connected.

steps.title: Check Power Cable and Adapters
steps.instructions: Inspect cables for frays, kinks, or damage. Swap with a verified working cable if available. Reason: Damaged power cords can reduce voltage to the pump, slowing or preventing charging.

steps.title: Confirm Battery Seating
steps.instructions: Remove the battery (if removable) and reinstall, ensuring it clicks into place. Reason: A loosely seated battery may show charge errors.

steps.title: Review Pump Indicators
steps.instructions: Observe battery icons or status LEDs when on dock. Look for flashing patterns indicating error codes. Reason: B. Braun pumps display diagnostic info via LEDs; this guides next steps.

steps.title: Perform Battery Calibration / Pump Reset
steps.instructions: If the pump supports a soft reset or battery recalibration, follow manufacturer guidelines. Reason: Sometimes the pump misreads battery state, showing rapid depletion incorrectly.

steps.title: Swap Battery (if available)
steps.instructions: Replace with a known working battery. Reason: Confirms if issue is pump-related or battery-specific.

CCR.Complaint: Pump battery will not charge and drains rapidly; pump disconnected from dock.
CCR.Cause: Verified power source, docking station, and cables; battery correctly seated. Pump battery continues to deplete quickly, suggesting possible internal battery or charging circuit failure.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent to biomedical repair for evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Final device status: Out of Service

title: B. Braun Space Infusion Pump System - Communication Failure to EMR / Drug Library Server
description: Troubleshooting guide for B. Braun Space Infusion Pumps that cannot communicate with EMR or drug library servers, focusing on external checks.

steps.title: Confirm Device Power and Operation
steps.instructions: Ensure the pump powers on fully and completes self-tests without errors. A pump not passing self-test may indicate broader hardware issues affecting communication.

steps.title: Check Network Connectivity
steps.instructions: Verify the Ethernet or Wi-Fi connection is secure. If using Ethernet, confirm the cable is seated and not damaged. If using Wi-Fi, confirm the network credentials and signal strength.

steps.title: Verify Network Settings on Pump
steps.instructions: Confirm IP address, subnet mask, gateway, and DNS are configured correctly. Ping the server from the pump (if available) or confirm the network port is accessible.

steps.title: Confirm Server Availability
steps.instructions: Verify the EMR or drug library server is online and responding. Check for scheduled maintenance or network outages that may impact connectivity.

steps.title: Check Firewall and Security Settings
steps.instructions: Ensure the pump’s IP address is allowed through network firewalls. Verify port requirements are met for communication with the EMR or drug library server.

steps.title: Test with Alternate Device or Port
steps.instructions: Connect another pump or device to the same network connection to rule out port or switch issues. Swap network cables if necessary.

steps.title: Restart Devices
steps.instructions: Restart the pump and, if possible, the network switch or EMR interface module to clear temporary communication errors.

CCR.Complaint: Pump unable to communicate with EMR/drug library; alerts displayed on interface.
CCR.Cause: Observed network connectivity issues; pump IP settings confirmed correct; server reachable from another device.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service and sent to Clinical Engineering bench for evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Any error messages displayed on the pump interface
Final device status: Out of Service

title: B. Braun Space Infusion Pump System - Incorrect Flow Rate / Rate Deviation Alarms
description: Guide for troubleshooting flow rate and rate deviation alarms on B. Braun Space Infusion Pumps, focusing on external causes.

steps.title: Verify Pump Programming
steps.instructions: Confirm the infusion rate and volume are correctly entered on the pump. Ensure the correct drug library profile is selected if using the integrated library. Why: User entry errors are a common cause of flow rate alarms.

steps.title: Check IV Line and Tubing
steps.instructions: Inspect the IV line for kinks, clamps, or occlusions. Confirm the correct tubing set or cassette is installed for the intended flow rate. Why: Misplaced tubing or blocked lines can alter flow rates.

steps.title: Ensure Proper Cassette Seating
steps.instructions: Remove and reseat the cassette, ensuring it clicks into place without resistance. Examine for any damage, debris, or residue that may prevent proper sensor detection. Why: Improper cassette placement can trigger rate deviation alarms.

steps.title: Check for Air or Bubbles
steps.instructions: Inspect the line for air bubbles or partially collapsed bags that can affect flow. Why: Air in the line can cause inconsistent flow and alarms.

steps.title: Verify Pump Orientation
steps.instructions: Ensure the pump is positioned upright on a stable surface or properly docked in the station. Why: Some pumps have flow sensors sensitive to tilt or movement.

steps.title: Inspect Power Source and Battery
steps.instructions: Confirm the pump is receiving stable power from the dock or outlet. If running on battery, ensure sufficient charge. Why: Voltage fluctuations can affect pump performance and alarms.

steps.title: Reset the Pump if Safe
steps.instructions: If external checks are complete, power cycle the pump. Reprogram and attempt a test infusion with water or saline if allowed by protocol. Why: Software glitches can sometimes cause false rate deviation alarms.

CCR.Complaint: Pump displays rate deviation alarm; infusion rate does not match programmed value.
CCR.Cause: IV tubing kinked and cassette not fully seated; external check completed.
CCR.Resolution: Corrected tubing placement, reseated cassette, and test infusion ran within acceptable limits. Pump returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Air in line or bag collapse noted

title: B. Braun Space Infusion Pump System - Pressure Sensor Fault / Line Not Detected
description: Guide for troubleshooting pressure sensor faults or line detection issues on B. Braun Space Infusion Pumps

steps.title: Verify Line Installation
steps.instructions: Ensure the IV line is fully seated in the pump channel. Confirm the correct tubing type is used (B. Braun-approved). Improper seating or wrong tubing often triggers “line not detected” errors.

steps.title: Inspect Tubing for Kinks or Obstructions
steps.instructions: Check the entire length of the line for kinks, clots, or air bubbles. Even minor obstruction can cause pressure sensor alarms.

steps.title: Check Pump Door / Sensor Engagement
steps.instructions: Open and close the pump door fully; listen for the click that indicates the door sensor engages. A misaligned door can prevent the pressure sensor from detecting the line.

steps.title: Examine Pressure Sensor Area
steps.instructions: Look for debris, residue, or moisture on the sensor or tubing path. Clean gently with approved wipes if needed; contamination can mimic a fault.

steps.title: Cycle Power / Reboot Pump
steps.instructions: Power off the pump, wait 10 seconds, and power on. Re-insert the line after reboot. This resets sensor calibration.

steps.title: Try a Known Good Line
steps.instructions: Replace the line with a new, compatible B. Braun line. If the fault clears, the issue was likely tubing-related.

steps.title: Inspect Docking Station / Power Connection (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Ensure pump is seated correctly in dock or connected to AC power. Some sensor errors appear when the pump is underpowered.

CCR.Complaint: Pump displays ‘pressure sensor fault’ and does not detect IV line.
CCR.Cause: Line correctly seated, tubing unobstructed, door and sensor area clean, reboot and line swap performed; fault persisted.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent to Biomedical Engineering for repair.

helpfulDetails: Final device status: Out of Service

title: B. Braun Space Infusion Pump System - Alarms Not Silenced / Alarm Tone Stuck
description: Guide for troubleshooting B. Braun Space Infusion Pumps when alarm tones persist and cannot be silenced, emphasizing patient safety and external cause resolution.

steps.title: Verify patient safety and alarm context
steps.instructions: Confirm the alarm is not indicating a true clinical issue (occlusion, empty bag, air-in-line). If the patient is connected, ensure another pump or monitoring device is in place before silencing attempts.

steps.title: Check the Alarm Silence Button
steps.instructions: Press the designated Alarm Silence button firmly. Ensure the button is not physically stuck or obstructed. Observe if the alarm temporarily silences or resets.

steps.title: Inspect the Keypad and Control Knob
steps.instructions: Verify that all keypad buttons and the rotary knob are responsive. If buttons are unresponsive, clean around buttons gently; residue can prevent proper actuation.

steps.title: Confirm Volume and Tone Settings
steps.instructions: Access the pump menu to verify the Alarm Volume is not set to maximum or muted incorrectly. Reset tone settings if accessible; sometimes persistent tone occurs due to corrupted user settings.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Pump
steps.instructions: Safely stop infusion if possible. Remove pump from power/docking station. Wait 10–15 seconds, then reconnect or power back on. Observe if the alarm tone stops after reboot.

steps.title: Check for Active Alarms or Messages
steps.instructions: Ensure no hidden alarms remain on screen (some may persist in message logs). Clear any queued or secondary alarms per on-screen instructions.

CCR.Complaint: Pump alarm cannot be silenced; tone continues indefinitely.
CCR.Cause: Alarm Silence button responsive; volume settings and power cycle performed; alarm persists.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent to Biomedical Engineering for bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Confirm if other pumps in the unit have normal alarm behavior.
Record if the keypad or buttons feel sticky or unresponsive.
Note the last infusion settings and any software version displayed.
Document power/docking station used and if volume settings were reset.

title: B. Braun Space Infusion Pump System - Software Freeze / Firmware Update Fails
description: Guide for troubleshooting software freezes and failed firmware updates on B. Braun Space Infusion Pumps.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the pump from an active infusion if possible. Move the patient to a backup pump before attempting troubleshooting. This prevents interruptions to therapy during troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Power Supply and Battery
steps.instructions: Confirm the pump is connected to a reliable AC source or fully charged battery. Unstable or insufficient power can cause freezes or failed updates. Swap power cords or test outlet if needed.

steps.title: Restart the Device
steps.instructions: Power down the pump completely. Wait 30 seconds, then power it back on. This resolves temporary software glitches in many cases.

steps.title: Verify Update Source and File Integrity
steps.instructions: Ensure firmware update files are complete and obtained from B. Braun’s official source. Check for corrupted files or interrupted downloads. Using a faulty update file often causes update failure or software lock.

steps.title: Disconnect Non-essential Accessories
steps.instructions: Remove IV sets, cables, and docking stations if not required. This reduces the chance of external hardware conflicts during the update.

steps.title: Attempt Firmware Update Again
steps.instructions: Follow B. Braun instructions exactly. Ensure the pump remains powered and connected for the full update duration. Avoid multitasking on the same network that may disrupt connectivity.

steps.title: Observe for Error Codes or Messages
steps.instructions: Record any codes displayed. Note whether the pump freezes during boot, update initiation, or during the update process.

CCR.Complaint: Pump froze during operation, firmware update will not complete.
CCR.Cause: Observed repeated software freeze after update attempt; verified power and network stability; update file integrity confirmed.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for firmware reflash/repair.

helpfulDetails: Power source tested: AC and battery.
Update file checked for integrity and official source.
Pump freeze behavior observed (during boot, update start, or mid-update).
Final device status: Out of Service, sent for repair.

title: B. Braun Outlook 400ES Infusion Pump - Pump Will Not Power On or Fails Startup Self-Test
description: Troubleshooting no-power, failed startup, battery, AC power, and startup self-test issues on the B. Braun Outlook 400ES infusion pump.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the pump is connected to a patient, do not troubleshoot it while therapy is active. Transfer the infusion to another approved pump according to clinical policy before continuing. Expected outcome: The patient is safely supported by another infusion device. Why it matters: A pump that will not power on or cannot pass startup checks should not be trusted for medication or fluid delivery.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Press the power button and observe the pump behavior. Check whether the pump does not respond at all, starts and shuts down, displays an error during startup, fails a self-test, only works on AC power, only works on battery power, or beeps but does not complete startup. Expected outcome: The failure mode is confirmed before parts or accessories are blamed.

steps.title: Check the AC Outlet
steps.instructions: Plug another approved device or outlet tester into the same wall outlet. Expected outcome: The outlet provides proper power. If the outlet is dead, damaged, loose, or controlled by a wall switch, move the pump to a known-good outlet and retest. If the pump powers on normally, stop troubleshooting and document the outlet issue.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord
steps.instructions: Check the power cord for cuts, bent prongs, loose strain relief, cracked insulation, or signs of overheating. Confirm the cord is fully seated at the pump and wall outlet. Expected outcome: The cord is intact and securely connected. If the cord is damaged, remove it from use and replace it with an approved compatible cord.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Power Cord
steps.instructions: Connect the Outlook 400ES to AC power using a known-good compatible power cord. Expected outcome: The pump powers on and begins normal startup. If the pump powers on with the replacement cord, the original cord was the likely cause. Stop and document the replacement.

steps.title: Check for Charging or AC Power Indicators
steps.instructions: With the pump connected to AC power, observe whether any charging, AC, or display indicator appears. Expected outcome: The pump shows some sign that external power is present. If there is no indication at all, the issue may involve the power cord, inlet, fuse, internal power supply, or battery/power management circuit.

steps.title: Inspect the AC Power Inlet
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the pump’s power inlet for looseness, cracks, bent contacts, debris, liquid residue, or burn marks. Do not insert tools into the inlet. Expected outcome: The inlet is clean, secure, and undamaged. If the inlet is loose, damaged, discolored, or smells burnt, remove the pump from service and send it for repair.

steps.title: Check Battery Condition
steps.instructions: If the pump shows signs of life only when connected to AC power, allow it to charge per facility process and retest startup. Expected outcome: The pump starts normally after charging. If the pump shuts down immediately when unplugged, does not retain charge, or reports a battery-related failure, the battery may be depleted, aged, disconnected, or failed.

steps.title: Test Startup on AC Power
steps.instructions: Connect the pump to a known-good outlet using a known-good power cord. Power the pump on and allow it to complete startup without pressing other buttons. Expected outcome: The pump completes its startup self-test without alarm or shutdown. If the pump passes startup on AC power, document the result and continue with required functional checks before returning it to service.

steps.title: Test Startup on Battery Power
steps.instructions: After confirming the pump operates on AC power, disconnect AC power and attempt startup on battery power if facility policy allows. Expected outcome: The pump starts and remains powered on using battery. If it fails only on battery, suspect battery condition or battery power path rather than a general no-power issue.

steps.title: Check for Stuck or Damaged Keypad Buttons
steps.instructions: Inspect the power button and front keypad area for sticking, cracking, fluid residue, or physical damage. Expected outcome: Buttons move normally and are not stuck. A stuck key can cause startup failure, self-test failure, or abnormal boot behavior. If keypad damage or fluid intrusion is suspected, remove the pump from service.

steps.title: Inspect for Fluid Intrusion or Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Check the case, display, keypad, power inlet, battery area, pole clamp area, and seams for cracks, impact damage, residue, corrosion, or signs of cleaning-fluid entry. Expected outcome: No evidence of damage or contamination is found. If fluid intrusion, impact damage, smoke smell, unusual heat, or corrosion is present, do not continue operational testing. Remove the pump from service.

steps.title: Note Any Startup Error Message
steps.instructions: If the pump powers on but fails startup self-test, record the exact displayed message, alarm tone, or failure code. Expected outcome: The failure is documented clearly for bench evaluation or vendor support. Do not clear or ignore repeated startup self-test failures unless the cause is confirmed and corrected.

steps.title: Power Cycle Once After External Checks
steps.instructions: Disconnect AC power, wait briefly, reconnect to known-good AC power, and attempt startup again. Expected outcome: The pump completes startup normally. If the same failure returns, treat it as a repeatable device fault rather than a one-time startup interruption.

steps.title: Perform Required Safety and Functional Checks Before Return to Service
steps.instructions: If the issue was corrected by outlet change, power cord replacement, charging, or cleaning of an external obstruction, perform the facility-required inspection, electrical safety check if applicable, battery check, and basic pump operational verification. Expected outcome: The Outlook 400ES passes required checks before being returned to clinical use. If it cannot pass startup self-test or any required verification, do not return it to service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the B. Braun Outlook 400ES infusion pump would not power on and failed to complete startup when connected to AC power.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting confirmed the wall outlet and power cord were functional, but the pump showed no reliable startup response and failed repeated power-on checks.
CCR.Resolution: Pump was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation due to suspected internal power or startup self-test failure.

helpfulDetails: Startup error message or alarm behavior
Whether the pump worked on AC, battery, both, or neither
Unusual heat, smell, smoke, or discoloration

title: B. Braun Outlook 400ES Infusion Pump - Door Latch, Cassette Loading, or Set Recognition Error
description: Troubleshooting cassette loading, door latch, and set recognition errors caused by tubing position, latch obstruction, or pump door issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Outlook 400ES is connected to a patient and the door, cassette, or set recognition error prevents reliable infusion, stop using that pump. Transfer the infusion to another verified pump according to clinical policy before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The patient is not dependent on a pump that may fail to recognize the administration set correctly. Why it matters: Door and cassette recognition issues can affect flow control, occlusion detection, and safe medication delivery.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Error or Symptom
steps.instructions: Power the pump on and observe the exact message or behavior reported by staff. Check whether the issue occurs: During cassette loading; When closing the door; At startup; When attempting to start an infusion; Intermittently during handling; Only with a specific tubing set. Expected outcome: The failure is confirmed or the pump operates normally during initial inspection. If the issue cannot be reproduced, document the staff report and continue with external checks.

steps.title: Inspect the Administration Set Compatibility
steps.instructions: Verify that the installed IV set is the correct type approved for the B. Braun Outlook 400ES. Look for incorrect, expired, damaged, or incompatible tubing sets. Expected outcome: A correct, undamaged, compatible set is being used. If the wrong set is found, replace it with the correct set and retest. If the issue clears, return the pump only after confirming proper operation.

steps.title: Remove and Reload the Cassette or Tubing Set
steps.instructions: Open the pump door and remove the cassette or tubing set. Reload it carefully, making sure the tubing is seated fully in the cassette channel and not twisted, stretched, pinched, or sitting outside the intended path. Close the door firmly without forcing it. Expected outcome: The set loads smoothly, the door closes normally, and the pump recognizes the set. If reloading resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document the loading-related cause.

steps.title: Check for Door Latch Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the door latch area, cassette seating area, and tubing path for visible obstruction. Look for: Dried fluid residue; Tape; Medication buildup; Broken plastic pieces; Foreign material; Misrouted tubing; Bent or damaged set components. Clean external residue using facility-approved cleaning procedures. Do not flood the pump or force fluid into openings. Expected outcome: The door and latch area are clean and free of obstruction. If an obstruction is removed and the pump recognizes the set normally, document the cause and retest.

steps.title: Inspect the Door for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Check the pump door for cracks, looseness, hinge damage, latch wear, or uneven closing. Gently close the door and confirm it aligns evenly with the pump housing. Expected outcome: The door closes evenly and latches securely without excessive force. If the door does not latch securely or appears damaged, remove the pump from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Administration Set
steps.instructions: Install a new, known-good, compatible set. Close the door and attempt normal setup using standard test fluid or approved test conditions. Expected outcome: The pump recognizes the known-good set and allows setup to continue. If the known-good set works, the original set was likely misloaded, damaged, or defective. If the known-good set fails, continue troubleshooting the pump.

steps.title: Check for Repeated or Intermittent Recognition Failure
steps.instructions: With the known-good set installed, gently handle the pump in a normal-use manner without excessive force. Observe whether the error returns when the door is touched, the set is repositioned, or the pump is moved. Expected outcome: The pump continues to recognize the set consistently. If recognition is intermittent, suspect a worn latch, door sensor issue, cassette detection issue, or internal recognition failure. Remove the pump from service.

steps.title: Verify Pump Response After Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Turn the pump off, wait briefly, and power it back on. Reload the known-good set and confirm whether the same error returns. Expected outcome: The pump boots normally and recognizes the set after proper loading. If the error returns after power cycling with a known-good set, remove the device from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check
steps.instructions: If the pump recognizes the set, perform a basic operational check per facility procedure. Confirm: Door remains latched; Set stays recognized; No door or cassette error appears; Pump starts without abnormal alarms; No visible leakage, unusual noise, or physical looseness is present. Expected outcome: The pump operates normally through setup and basic function testing. If all checks pass, the pump may be returned to service per department policy.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported that the B. Braun Outlook 400ES displayed a cassette/set recognition error after loading the IV set and would not start the infusion.
CCR.Cause: Found tubing not fully seated in the cassette path, preventing the door from closing and the set from being recognized correctly.
CCR.Resolution: Reloaded the compatible administration set, confirmed the door latched securely, verified the pump recognized the set, and completed basic functional testing.

helpfulDetails: Whether issue occurred during loading, startup, or infusion setup
Whether a known-good set was tested
Any residue, obstruction, or fluid contamination found
Whether the issue was intermittent or constant
Whether the pump passed basic functional testing
Final device status: returned to service or removed for repair

title: B. Braun Outlook 400ES Infusion Pump - Air-in-Line Alarm with No Visible Air Present
description: Troubleshooting air-in-line alarms caused by tubing setup, sensor contamination, cassette seating, fluid path issues, or pump detection problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Outlook 400ES is connected to a patient and an air-in-line alarm is active, do not silence and continue infusion without verifying the fluid path. Stop the infusion according to facility procedure and move the patient to another pump if therapy must continue. Expected outcome: Patient therapy is maintained safely without depending on a pump in alarm. Why it matters: Air-in-line alarms are safety alarms. Even if no air is visible, the alarm must be treated as real until the line and pump are checked.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Alarm
steps.instructions: Verify that the displayed or reported alarm is specifically related to air-in-line, not occlusion, upstream pressure, cassette, door, or flow error. Check whether the alarm occurs: Immediately after setup; During infusion; After moving the pump; After changing the bag, tubing, or cassette; Intermittently with no clear pattern. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as an air detection problem. If the alarm clears after correcting a different alarm condition, stop troubleshooting and document the actual cause.

steps.title: Inspect the IV Line From Bag to Patient
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the entire tubing path, not just the area near the pump. Look for: Small bubbles at connectors; Air trapped near injection ports; Air near the drip chamber; Loose connections; Empty or nearly empty fluid container; Kinks or tension on the tubing. Expected outcome: No visible air, loose fittings, or fluid path problems are found. If air is found, remove the pump from patient use until the line is properly reprimed or replaced by clinical staff. Stop troubleshooting once normal operation is confirmed.

steps.title: Check the Tubing and Cassette Installation
steps.instructions: Open the pump door only when safe and appropriate. Verify the administration set or cassette is fully seated and routed correctly through the pump. Check for: Tubing not fully seated in the air detector area; Cassette not latched correctly; Tubing twisted, stretched, or pinched; Incorrect set type; Damaged, flattened, or worn tubing segment. Expected outcome: Tubing and cassette are installed correctly with no mechanical distortion. Why it matters: Air sensors depend on correct tubing position. Misalignment can trigger false or intermittent air-in-line alarms.

steps.title: Replace the Administration Set
steps.instructions: If no visible air is present and the alarm continues, have clinical staff install and prime a new compatible administration set. Use a known-good, properly primed set and fluid container. Expected outcome: The pump runs without an air-in-line alarm. If replacing the set resolves the issue, the likely cause was tubing deformation, poor priming, a set defect, or trapped microbubbles. Stop troubleshooting and document the set-related finding.

steps.title: Inspect the Air Detector Area
steps.instructions: With the pump removed from patient use, inspect the air detector channel and surrounding tubing path. Look for: Fluid residue; Medication buildup; Dust or debris; Cracked plastic; Foreign material in the sensor path; Signs of impact or damage near the door or cassette area. Clean only according to approved facility and manufacturer cleaning procedures. Expected outcome: The sensor area is clean, dry, and physically intact. If residue or debris is removed and the pump passes testing, stop troubleshooting and document the cleaning action.

steps.title: Check Door Closure and Mechanical Fit
steps.instructions: Close the pump door and confirm it latches normally without looseness, binding, or unusual resistance. Gently inspect for broken hinges, worn latch points, or poor cassette compression. Expected outcome: Door closes securely and holds the set in the correct position. Why it matters: A loose or misaligned door can allow the tubing or cassette to sit incorrectly in the air detector path.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Setup
steps.instructions: On the bench, test the Outlook 400ES using: Known-good compatible tubing; Properly primed line; Adequate fluid level; Stable power source; No patient connection. Start an infusion at a typical test rate and observe for alarm recurrence. Expected outcome: Pump operates without an air-in-line alarm. If the pump alarms again with a known-good setup and no visible air, the issue is likely pump-related rather than set-related.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Behavior
steps.instructions: While testing safely on the bench, observe whether the alarm appears when: The door is touched; The pump is moved; The tubing is lightly repositioned; The cassette area is gently disturbed; The pump warms up during operation. Do not force the door, tubing, or cassette. Expected outcome: Alarm behavior is stable and reproducible. Why it matters: Intermittent air-in-line alarms may indicate sensor alignment, door fit, connector seating, or internal detection circuitry issues.

steps.title: Review Service History
steps.instructions: Check prior work orders for repeated air-in-line alarms, door latch repairs, fluid intrusion, impact damage, or recurring set-detection complaints. Expected outcome: Repeat history is identified or ruled out. If the pump has repeated air-in-line complaints with multiple tubing sets, escalate the pump for repair evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the B. Braun Outlook 400ES repeatedly displayed an air-in-line alarm even though no air was visible in the IV tubing.
CCR.Cause: Bench testing found the alarm persisted with a known-good primed administration set, indicating the issue was not caused by visible air, tubing setup, or fluid container condition.
CCR.Resolution: Pump was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for repair evaluation due to suspected air detector or cassette-position sensing fault.

helpfulDetails: Whether the alarm occurred during patient use or bench testing
Whether visible air was found in the line
Air detector area clean or contaminated
Any fluid residue near the sensor path
Whether alarm occurred with known-good tubing
Whether alarm was intermittent or constant

title: B. Braun Outlook 400ES Infusion Pump - Occlusion Alarm During Normal Infusion
description: Troubleshooting occlusion alarms caused by tubing restriction, clamp position, pressure settings, IV site issues, or pump setup problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the pump is actively infusing and an occlusion alarm occurs, notify clinical staff and ensure the patient’s therapy is not interrupted without clinical awareness. Do not clear the alarm repeatedly without confirming the cause. Expected outcome: Patient therapy is protected while the alarm condition is evaluated. Why it matters: An occlusion alarm may indicate that medication or fluid is not reaching the patient.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Alarm
steps.instructions: Review the pump display and confirm the alarm is an occlusion alarm, not air-in-line, door open, battery, keep vein open, or infusion complete. Note whether the alarm is: Upstream occlusion; Downstream occlusion; Intermittent during infusion; Immediate after start; Occurring only with a specific channel, tubing set, or medication setup. Expected outcome: The alarm type and timing are confirmed before troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: Stop Troubleshooting on the Patient When Needed
steps.instructions: If the issue cannot be quickly verified externally, have clinical staff move the infusion to another pump or line as appropriate. Expected outcome: Clinical therapy continues safely on a reliable setup. If this resolves the clinical situation, continue evaluating the removed pump separately.

steps.title: Inspect the IV Tubing Path
steps.instructions: Trace the tubing from the fluid container to the patient connection. Check for: Closed roller clamp; Kinked tubing; Tubing caught under the pump door; Tubing pinched by bed rails, blankets, or patient positioning; Tubing routed too tightly around the pump; Extension set restriction. Expected outcome: Tubing should be open, straight, properly routed, and free of visible restriction. If a kink or closed clamp is found, correct it, restart per clinical policy, and stop if the alarm clears.

steps.title: Check the Fluid Source and Upstream Side
steps.instructions: Verify the fluid container has adequate volume and is hanging properly. Inspect the spike, drip chamber, venting if applicable, and upstream tubing for restriction. Expected outcome: Fluid should flow freely to the pump when the upstream path is open. Why it matters: Upstream restriction can trigger occlusion-like behavior or cause abnormal pumping conditions.

steps.title: Check the Downstream Side
steps.instructions: Inspect the tubing from the pump outlet to the patient. Look for: Closed slide clamp; Kinked extension tubing; Stopcock turned off; Injection cap obstruction; Filter restriction; Tight or bent tubing near the IV site; Patient arm position causing flow restriction. Expected outcome: The downstream path should be open and unrestricted. If a closed clamp or stopcock is found, correct the issue and stop if normal infusion resumes.

steps.title: Have Clinical Staff Assess the IV Site
steps.instructions: Ask nursing or authorized clinical staff to verify IV patency. Do not attempt to clinically assess or manipulate the patient’s IV site unless within your role and facility policy. Expected outcome: The IV site is confirmed patent, or the infusion is moved to another access point. Why it matters: A good pump can alarm correctly if the patient line is infiltrated, positional, or occluded.

steps.title: Verify Tubing Is Loaded Correctly
steps.instructions: Remove the pump from patient use before opening the door for equipment inspection. Check that the administration set is properly seated in the pumping mechanism and sensor areas. Look for stretched, flattened, damaged, or incorrectly installed tubing. Expected outcome: Tubing should sit correctly in the pump path without being pinched or misaligned. If improper loading is found, reload with a new approved set and test again.

steps.title: Use a Known-Good B. Braun-Compatible Administration Set
steps.instructions: Replace the tubing set with a known-good, correct administration set approved for the Outlook 400ES. Do not reuse suspect tubing if it appears damaged, stretched, contaminated, or restricted. Expected outcome: If the original set was restricted or damaged, the occlusion alarm should not return with a known-good set.

steps.title: Check Occlusion Pressure Setting or Therapy Configuration
steps.instructions: Review the pump setup with clinical staff to confirm the selected therapy, rate, and pressure/occlusion settings are appropriate for the infusion. Expected outcome: Pump settings should match the intended clinical use and facility policy. Why it matters: A low occlusion pressure limit or inappropriate setup may alarm during a clinically normal infusion.

steps.title: Test the Pump Away From the Patient
steps.instructions: Remove the Outlook 400ES from patient use and test it with: Known-good tubing; Appropriate test fluid setup; Open downstream path; Controlled test container or outlet setup. Start an infusion and observe whether the occlusion alarm occurs under normal unrestricted flow. Expected outcome: The pump should infuse without occlusion alarms when the fluid path is open. If the pump works normally during bench testing, the original issue was likely related to tubing, setup, pressure setting, or patient line restriction.

steps.title: Check for External Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the pump door, latch, tubing channel, pressure sensor area, and exterior housing. Look for: Sticky medication residue; Cracked door or latch; Damaged tubing guide; Debris in the tubing path; Signs of fluid intrusion; Unusual heat, smell, or noise. Expected outcome: The pump should be clean, intact, and free of visible damage. If contamination is found in the tubing path, clean according to facility procedure and retest.

steps.title: Repeat Test After Cleaning and Setup Correction
steps.instructions: After correcting external causes, repeat a controlled infusion test. Confirm: No occlusion alarm occurs; Pump runs at the selected rate; Door and latch remain secure; Alarm clears normally; No abnormal noise or mechanical behavior is present. Expected outcome: Pump operates normally without false occlusion alarms. If the pump passes testing, document the findings and return to service according to facility policy.

steps.title: Remove From Service if Alarm Repeats Without Restriction
steps.instructions: If the Outlook 400ES continues to alarm for occlusion with known-good tubing, open clamps, correct setup, and unrestricted flow, remove it from service. Expected outcome: Suspect internal pressure sensing or pumping mechanism issues are escalated for repair.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported that the B. Braun Outlook 400ES repeatedly displayed an occlusion alarm during a normal infusion.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found the downstream extension tubing was kinked near the bed rail, causing flow restriction.
CCR.Resolution: Tubing was repositioned, infusion was restarted by clinical staff, pump was tested with known-good tubing, and no further occlusion alarms occurred.

helpfulDetails: Upstream or downstream alarm if identified
Infusion rate and therapy type if provided
IV site assessed by clinical staff
Any residue, fluid intrusion, or physical damage
Whether alarm repeated during bench testing

title: B. Braun Outlook 400ES Infusion Pump - Battery Will Not Charge Or Runtime Is Too Short
description: Troubleshooting Outlook 400ES battery charging, short runtime, poor charge retention, or power-source related battery complaints.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the pump is connected to a patient and battery operation is unreliable, do not continue troubleshooting on the active patient. Transfer the infusion to another verified pump or connect the pump to approved AC power if clinically appropriate and safe. Expected outcome: Patient therapy is maintained without depending on a questionable battery. Why it matters: Infusion pumps must maintain reliable power during transport, power interruptions, or bedside use.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Battery Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what was observed: Pump would not charge; Battery icon did not increase; Pump shut down quickly on battery; Low battery alarm occurred shortly after unplugging; Pump only works on AC power; Battery runtime is shorter than expected. Expected outcome: The complaint is clearly identified before testing. If the issue was caused by normal low battery use and the pump charges normally afterward, document and return to service if all checks pass.

steps.title: Inspect the Pump Exterior Before Power Testing
steps.instructions: Check the pump housing, battery compartment area, power connector, pole clamp area, and keypad/display for damage, cracks, fluid intrusion, residue, or signs of impact. Expected outcome: No physical damage, contamination, swelling, odor, or evidence of liquid exposure is found. If damage, fluid intrusion, unusual heat, or burnt smell is present, remove the pump from service and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Verify the AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Plug the pump into a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Confirm the outlet has power and is not controlled by a wall switch, power strip, loose receptacle, or failed emergency power circuit. Expected outcome: The pump powers normally from AC power and indicates AC connection or charging behavior. If the pump charges normally from a different outlet, the issue is external to the pump. Stop and document the outlet-related finding.

steps.title: Check the Power Cord and Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the AC power cord for cuts, bent blades, loose strain relief, cracked insulation, or intermittent connection. Reseat the cord firmly at the pump and outlet. If available, test with a known-good compatible power cord. Expected outcome: The pump detects AC power consistently without flickering, intermittent charging, or power loss when the cord is gently repositioned. If a known-good cord resolves the issue, replace the defective cord and stop.

steps.title: Allow Adequate Charging Time
steps.instructions: Leave the Outlook 400ES connected to AC power long enough for the battery to recover from a low or depleted state. Confirm that the charge indicator, battery icon, or charging status behaves normally. Expected outcome: Battery charge level increases over time, and low battery indications clear. If the battery was deeply discharged and recovers after charging, verify runtime before returning the pump to service.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent AC Detection
steps.instructions: With the pump connected to AC power, gently move the cord near the outlet end and pump connection while watching for AC power loss, display changes, alarms, or charge interruption. Do not stress the connector or force movement. Expected outcome: AC power remains stable. If AC detection drops in and out, remove the pump from service for power inlet, connector, or internal power-path evaluation.

steps.title: Verify Battery Runtime Under Safe Test Conditions
steps.instructions: After charging, disconnect AC power and operate the pump on battery in a non-patient test setup according to facility practice. Monitor for early low battery alarms, unexpected shutdown, rapid battery indicator drop, or failure to remain powered. Expected outcome: The pump operates on battery without premature shutdown or immediate low battery alarm. If runtime is too short after a full charge, suspect an aged or failed battery. Remove from service for battery replacement or bench evaluation.

steps.title: Check Battery Age and Maintenance History
steps.instructions: Review service history for the battery replacement date, prior low runtime complaints, repeated charging issues, or failed battery tests. Expected outcome: Battery age and maintenance history support reliable operation. If the battery is old, has repeated complaints, or fails runtime testing, replace it per facility procedure or send the pump for battery service.

steps.title: Perform Final Functional Verification
steps.instructions: After correcting an external issue or replacing a battery through the proper service process, verify: Pump powers on with AC; Pump recognizes AC power; Battery charges; Pump runs on battery; No power-related alarms occur; Display and keypad respond normally; Final safety and operational checks pass per facility procedure. Expected outcome: Pump passes functional checks and is safe to return to service. If any power, charging, or runtime issue remains, do not return the pump to clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the B. Braun Outlook 400ES battery would not hold a charge and the pump displayed a low battery alarm shortly after being unplugged.
CCR.Cause: Pump powered normally on AC, outlet and power cord tested good, but battery runtime was short after extended charging.
CCR.Resolution: Removed pump from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for battery replacement and bench verification before return to use.

helpfulDetails: AC power cord inspected or swapped
Battery icon or charge indicator behavior
Whether pump operates normally on AC power
Whether pump shuts down immediately on battery
Unusual heat, odor, damage, or fluid exposure
